/// (name bytes, value bytes, NGHTTP2 NV flags).
+/// One in-flight `writeBuffer` / `writeUtf8String` call. Mirrors an entry of
+  /// The JS `WriteWrap`. Its `oncomplete` runs once nghttp2 has framed every
+  /// byte this call contributed, which is what makes the write asynchronous
+  /// and therefore what lets `Http2Stream.write()` report backpressure.
+  /// Value of `outbound_written` right after this call's bytes were queued,

+  /// Total bytes of `pending_data` nghttp2 has framed. Writes complete as this
+  /// catches up with their `PendingWrite::end`, so it only advances when the
+  /// peer's flow-control window lets DATA out — that is the backpressure
+  /// signal. Cumulative rather than per-write remainders so a zero-length
+  /// write still completes behind the writes queued before it.
/// `SETTINGS_MAX_HEADER_LIST_SIZE` snapshotted from the session at stream
+  /// Queue `data` for the data provider and take ownership of `req` until
+  /// always reported to JS as asynchronous and its `oncomplete` is deferred to
+  /// `consume_outbound`. Completing it here instead (as this op used to) makes
+  /// every write look instantly drained, so `Http2Stream.write()` always
+  /// returns true and a producer never waits for `drain` — that is what let
+    let end = self.outbound_written.get() + data.len() as u64;
+    // SAFETY: session pointer is valid during stream lifetime
+    // SAFETY: isolate pointer is valid during session lifetime
+    // An empty write contributes no bytes, so no data provider pass will ever
+    // complete it; it is done as soon as everything queued ahead of it has
+    // been framed. Collect it now (the caller always schedules a send pass,
+    // which is what actually runs the completion) — otherwise `write('')`
+  /// Account for `amount` bytes of `pending_data` that nghttp2 has framed,
+  /// moving every write request they completed into `completed`.
+  /// The completions are handed back rather than run here because both callers
+  /// are nghttp2 callbacks driven by `mem_send`; the session runs them once
+  /// that pass is over and re-entering JS is safe.

+    // Nothing will drain this stream's pending_data now, so hand every queued

// The native writeUtf8String / writeBuffer ops queue bytes into
// nghttp2's per-stream pending_data and call resume_data. We then need
// a mem_send pass to actually frame and emit the DATA. Defer that pass
// data provider runs. With writable_ended observed at frame time,
// nghttp2 packs END_STREAM onto the trailing DATA frame instead of
// emitting a separate empty DATA(END_STREAM) frame
-    // still fires synchronously here (kLastWriteWasAsync = 0); only the

+    // drain pending writes synchronously before submitting RST_STREAM so the
// queued DATA frame still reaches the peer.
+    // Both ops keep the WriteWrap and only invoke its oncomplete once nghttp2
+    // has framed the bytes, so a queued write is asynchronous (as it is in
+    // Node, whose Http2Stream::DoWrite always defers to ClearOutgoing). That
+    // is what holds writableLength up while the peer's flow-control window is
+    // closed, making Http2Stream.write() return false and stalling the
+    // producer instead of letting pending_data grow without bound.
+    // afterWriteDispatched reads both fields right after the op returns, and
+    // kAfterAsyncWrite later credits kBytesWritten back to the write queue
+    // accounting that trackWriteState debits, so they have to agree.
@@ -898,6 +898,65 @@ Deno.test("[node/http2 client] connect with pre-created socket", {
+// A stream write only completes once nghttp2 has framed its bytes, so a peer
+// that stops reading holds the write outstanding instead of letting it buffer
+// without bound on the native side.
+    // The client leaves the response paused, so its flow-control window closes
+    // and nghttp2 stops accepting DATA. Write until the Writable pushes back.
+    // With the pre-fix synchronous completion, write() always returns true, so
+    // this loop never breaks and the test would hang until the runner's
+    // timeout rather than failing the assertions below — that timeout is the
+    // Nothing can drain the write while the window is shut, so its callback
+    // must stay outstanding rather than reporting a bogus instant completion.
+    assert(pending > 0, "write completed even though the peer never read");
+    // Reading reopens the window, which releases the write and the producer.
